title: comforting, bitter french jerusalem artichokes bourguignon
description: a hearty, comforting french dish featuring jerusalem artichokes in a rich, sugary-free bourguignon sauce, inspired by the classic boeuf bourguignon.

ingredients.name: jerusalem artichokes (4-6)
ingredients.notes: select artichokes with firm skin and no signs of sprouting.

ingredients.name: bacon
ingredients.notes: use a smoky, thick-cut bacon for best flavor.

ingredients.name: shallots
ingredients.notes: thinly slice the shallots and separate into rings.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.notes: mince the garlic and press into oil until fragrant.

ingredients.name: red wine
ingredients.notes: use a full-bodied burgundy for best flavor.

ingredients.name: miso paste
ingredients.notes: mix with 2 tablespoons red wine to create a marinade.

ingredients.name: olive oil
ingredients.notes: use a mild, fruity olive oil for sautéing.

ingredients.name: beef broth
ingredients.notes: use a high-quality, low-sodium broth for best flavor.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.notes: use a high-quality, low-sodium paste for best flavor.

ingredients.name: thyme
ingredients.notes: fresh thyme is best, but you can substitute with dried if necessary.

ingredients.name: rosemary
ingredients.notes: fresh rosemary is best, but you can substitute with dried if necessary.

instructions: heat the olive oil in a large Dutch oven over medium-high heat.
add the bacon and cook until browned, about 4 minutes.
remove the bacon and set aside.
add the shallots and cook until caramelized, about 8 minutes.
add the garlic and cook for 1 minute, pressing into the pan to release fragrance.
add the jerusalem artichokes, red wine, miso paste, and beef broth.
bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low and simmer for 20 minutes, or until the artichokes are tender.
add the tomato paste and stir to combine.
return the bacon to the pot and stir to combine.
add the thyme and rosemary and season with salt and black pepper to taste.
simmer the stew for an additional 10 minutes, or until the flavors have melded together and the meat is tender.
